content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>session/set_config_option是哪來的?</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-07-03</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content:
PROGRESS SUMMARY CHECKPOINT
===========================
Write progress notes of what was done, what was learned, and what's next. This is a checkpoint to capture progress so far. The session is ongoing - you may receive more requests and tool executions after this summary. Write "next_steps" as the current trajectory of work (what's actively being worked on or coming up next), not as post-session future work. Always write at least a minimal summary explaining current progress, even if work is still in early stages, so that users see a summary output tied to each request.

Claude's Full Response to User:
已 commit 到 `main`:

**`441ff16`** — fix: 還原 upstream merge 誤刪的 tool-observer seq 層
- 3 檔 `+131 / -22`
- 只含這 3 個源碼檔(未追蹤的 `output/` 未納入)
- 未 push(依慣例留給你決定推送時機)

---

## 這次工作總結

| 項目 | 狀態 |
|------|------|
| 兩天改動審查 | 發現 1 個 merge 靜默回退(tsc 隱形)+ 1 個資料漂移 |
| seq-tier observer 還原 | ✅ commit `441ff16`,check-tool-observer 9/9 |
| topic 分片資料漂移 | ✅ 已 rebuild,check-topic-match PASS(資料修復,無需 commit)|

要 push 的話再跟我說。另外提醒:`check-acp-model-effort` 對本機 Kiro CLI 仍會失敗(缺 `session/set_config_option`),那是 CLI 版本問題不是程式碼缺陷,無需處理。

Respond in this XML format:
<summary>
  <request>[Short title capturing the user's request AND the substance of what was discussed/done]</request>
  <investigated>[What has been explored so far? What was examined?]</investigated>
  <learned>[What have you learned about how things work?]</learned>
  <completed>[What work has been completed so far? What has shipped or changed?]</completed>
  <next_steps>[What are you actively working on or planning to work on next in this session?]</next_steps>
  <notes>[Additional insights or observations about the current progress]</notes>
</summary>

content.thinking:
The user is observing a git commit being made in the primary session. Let me analyze what was done:

1. **Working directory**: `G:\AI\telegram-kiro-bridge-main`
2. **Files modified**: 
   - `src/tool-observer.ts`
   - `src/provider/tool-loop.ts`
   - `src/sessionManager.ts`
3. **What happened**: A bugfix commit that restored the "tool-observer seq tier" that was accidentally deleted during an upstream merge.

From the commit message, I can extract:
- The issue: During upstream sync (commit d22a568), the entire file was replaced with upstream's simpler "rich-only observer", silently reverting the local "dual-tier design" (from commit a855fee)
- Impact: The ACP path (Kiro/Codex/Claude adapter) stopped writing observations.jsonl, lost tool_call event logs and tool name display (showing opaque toolCallId instead)
- Fix: Restored several functions and features:
  - In tool-observer.ts: Added back `recordSeqObservation`, `SeqObservationInput`, `TURN_MARKER`, `PrecedingSemantics`, and the `seq` field in Observation, plus exec-order marking in rich
  - In tool-loop.ts: BC-7 snapshots original args before RTK rewrite (no longer records as "rtk git status")
  - In sessionManager.ts: Restored `_toolTitles` (tool name resolution), `_recentToolNames`, `logEvent` in tool_call_update, `recordSeqObservation` (with activeProvider==="acp" guard), and TURN_MARKER in resetTurn; also fixed display to show tool names
- Verification: tsc/build clean; check-tool-observer 9/9 passed; all non-live smoke tests green

This is a **bugfix** type observation. The title should capture that this restores the seq-tier observation functionality that was lost in an upstream merge.
The upstream merge silently replaced the dual-tier observer with a simpler rich-only version, breaking the ACP adapter's ability to write observations.jsonl and display tool names. I've restored the seq-tier mechanism across all three files—recordSeqObservation functions, TURN_MARKER constants, and tool name resolution—which re-enables event logging and proper tool identification. All tests confirm the fix is solid.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Restored seq-tier tool observation layer lost in upstream merge</title>
  <subtitle>Upstream sync silently replaced dual-tier observer with simpler version, breaking ACP adapter's observation logging and tool name display</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>Upstream merge d22a568 replaced local dual-tier observer design with upstream's rich-only observer</fact>
    <fact>ACP adapter (Kiro/Codex/Claude) stopped writing observations.jsonl and displayed opaque toolCallId instead of tool names</fact>
    <fact>Restored recordSeqObservation, SeqObservationInput, TURN_MARKER, and PrecedingSemantics to src/tool-observer.ts</fact>
    <fact>Added tool name resolution (_toolTitles, _recentToolNames) and seq observation recording to src/sessionManager.ts with activeProvider==="acp" guard</fact>
    <fact>Modified src/provider/tool-loop.ts BC-7 to snapshot original args before RTK rewrite</fact>
    <fact>All verification passed: tsc/build clean, check-tool-observer 9/9, non-live smoke tests green</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>During upstream synchronization, the entire tool-observer file was replaced with a simpler "rich-only" version, silently reverting the local dual-tier observation design. This caused the ACP adapter path to stop recording tool observations to observations.jsonl and lose the ability to display tool names, showing only opaque toolCallId values instead. The fix restored the complete seq-tier observation infrastructure: added back recordSeqObservation and related types to tool-observer.ts with exec-order marking for the rich tier, restored tool name resolution and event logging in sessionManager.ts (guarded by activeProvider==="acp" check), and fixed tool-loop.ts to snapshot arguments before RTK rewrites. The local implementation is now a strict functional superset of upstream with zero feature loss. This restoration ensures that observation logging, tool call tracking, and proper tool name display work correctly across all adapter paths.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
    <concept>what-changed</concept>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>gotcha</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read></files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>src/tool-observer.ts</file>
    <file>src/provider/tool-loop.ts</file>
    <file>src/sessionManager.ts</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```

content.thinking:
The user is asking "session/set_config_option是哪來的?" (Where does session/set_config_option come from?)

The primary session is investigating this by:
1. First searching for all references to set_config_option in the codebase
2. Reading the acpClient.ts file to see how it's used
3. Checking config.ts and git history to see when it was introduced

The findings show:
- It's used in acpClient.ts to pin model and effort settings after session/new
- It was introduced in commit 57b3daa "feat: pin ACP model/effort via session/set_config_option" by tonykuo on 2026-07-02
- Another related commit c524d51 "feat: specialist model/effort pin via session/set_config_option" also on 2026-07-02
- These are LOCAL commits (author: tonykuo, not from upstream)
- The feature allows setting ACP session model/effort via the session/set_config_option ACP request
- It's needed because claude-agent-acp's CLI flags --model/--effort are ignored in ACP server mode
- Configuration comes from ACP_MODEL and ACP_EFFORT environment variables

This is a discovery observation - the user is learning about where a feature came from and how it works.

content.text:
```xml
<observation>
  <type>discovery</type>
  <title>session/set_config_option ACP request origin and purpose</title>
  <subtitle>Local feature added in commit 57b3daa to pin ACP model/effort settings, required because CLI flags ignored in server mode</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>session/set_config_option introduced in local commit 57b3daa on 2026-07-02 by tonykuo</fact>
    <fact>ACP client calls session/set_config_option after session/new to apply acpModel and acpEffort configuration</fact>
    <fact>Configuration values read from ACP_MODEL and ACP_EFFORT environment variables defined in src/config.ts</fact>
    <fact>Feature necessary because claude-agent-acp CLI flags --model and --effort are ignored in ACP server mode</fact>
    <fact>Related commit c524d51 added specialist model/effort pinning via same mechanism</fact>
    <fact>Implementation in src/acpClient.ts applyModelEffort() method applies model before effort (effort levels are model-specific)</fact>
    <fact>Failures are logged to stderr and swallowed to never abort session startup (best-effort approach)</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>The session/set_config_option ACP request was added locally in commit 57b3daa (2026-07-02) to solve a specific problem: claude-agent-acp ignores its CLI --model and --effort flags when running in ACP server mode. The bridge needed a way to reliably pin model and reasoning effort settings for interactive sessions. The solution uses the session/set_config_option ACP protocol method, called immediately after session/new, to explicitly set these configuration values. The acpClient reads ACP_MODEL (accepts fuzzy aliases like "fable-5") and ACP_EFFORT (must match exactly: "low"/"medium"/"high") from environment variables and applies them via best-effort requests that log failures but never abort. Model is applied before effort because adapters rebuild the effort option list based on the selected model. This is exclusively for interactive sessions; ephemeral sessions (scheduler, specialist, daily-intel) don't wire these options through.</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>how-it-works</concept>
    <concept>why-it-exists</concept>
    <concept>problem-solution</concept>
  </concepts>
  <files_read>
    <file>src/acpClient.ts</file>
    <file>src/config.ts</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified></files_modified>
</observation>
```

<summary>
  <request>User asked where session/set_config_option came from, triggering investigation into feature origin and purpose</request>
  <investigated>Searched codebase for all set_config_option references, examined acpClient.ts implementation, checked config.ts environment variables, and traced git history to identify introducing commits</investigated>
  <learned>session/set_config_option is a local feature added in commit 57b3daa (2026-07-02) to pin ACP model and effort settings after session creation; necessary because claude-agent-acp ignores CLI flags in server mode; reads from ACP_MODEL/ACP_EFFORT env vars; uses best-effort approach that never aborts startup on failure</learned>
  <completed>Committed seq-tier observer restoration (441ff16) with 3 files changed (+131/-22); identified session/set_config_option as local commit 57b3daa solving ACP server mode configuration issue</completed>
  <next_steps>Investigation complete for set_config_option origin question; awaiting next user request or direction</next_steps>
  <notes>The check-acp-model-effort script expects this feature but fails against local Kiro CLI because the CLI version doesn't support the ACP request - this is a CLI version limitation, not a code defect</notes>
</summary>
